title: Extending UNIQuE: Quantum Simulation Speedup for the HHL Algorithm
abstract: In an extension of the Unconventional Noiseless Intermediate Quantum Emulator, this work introduces a classical emulation of the quantum Harrow-Hassidim-Lloyd algorithm for sampling from the solution space of linear systems. The emulated HHL algorithm scales exponentially with the number of qubits required to represent the linear system, which is an advantage over the state vector simulation of the HHL algorithm, which scales exponentially as a function of both the size of the linear system and the magnitude of its largest (scaled) eigenvalue. We benchmark our emulator by comparing it with the Intel Quantum Simulator and demonstrate a runtime advantage for small linear systems.
summary: This paper introduces a new classical emulation method for the HHL quantum algorithm that scales more efficiently than standard state-vector simulation. By focusing on reproducing the final output distribution rather than simulating every intermediate gate, the authors eliminate the exponential cost associated with the precision of eigenvalue estimation. Benchmarks show that this emulator provides a significant speedup for small linear systems while maintaining high accuracy. This approach offers a more scalable way to verify and study the outcomes of HHL-type algorithms on classical hardware.
main_problem: The challenge of efficiently replicating the behavior of the HHL quantum algorithm on classical hardware, specifically overcoming the exponential scaling of traditional state-vector simulation with respect to the number of counting qubits.
main_result: The proposed emulation method achieves a runtime advantage over the Intel Quantum Simulator for small linear systems by removing the exponential dependence on the number of counting qubits used for eigenvalue accuracy.
method: An extension of the Unconventional Noiseless Intermediate Quantum Emulator (UNIQuE) that bypasses intermediate quantum circuit steps like QPE and instead directly computes the final probability distribution by diagonalizing the matrix A.
model_or_system: The HHL algorithm applied to an s-sparse, N x N invertible Hermitian matrix A, involving ancilla, solution, and clock registers.
key_observables: The probability distribution of the final state and the ratio of probabilities of measuring the ancilla qubit in the |1> state to estimate the solution vector.
important_parameters: The size of the linear system (N), the condition number (kappa), the error tolerance (epsilon), and the number of counting qubits (m).
main_assumptions: The study assumes a noiseless/perfect quantum computing environment and focuses on replicating the final sampling distribution rather than intermediate gate operations.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the HHL quantum circuit diagram; Figure 2 and 3 present histograms comparing simulation and emulation results for different qubit sizes to demonstrate accuracy and stability.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of HHL simulation, describes the proposed UNIQuE extension, details the emulation methodology, provides a comparative benchmark against the Intel Quantum Simulator, and concludes with error analysis and scaling results.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: A high-level overview of the HHL algorithm. The algorithm uses three quantum registers: a one-qubit ancilla register a, an n-qubit b register to encode the value of |b⟩(and later |x⟩), and an m-qubit clock register c. The algorithm begins by encoding |b⟩into b via the state-preparation unitary Ub. Next, the Quantum Phase Estimation (QPE) routine is used to apply the eigenvalues of A to b. This routine uses the Inverse Quantum Fourier Transform (IQFT) as a subroutine. Following this, an R(λ−1) rotation is used to invert the eigenvalues applied to b (provided that a is measured to be in the |1⟩state), and the Inverse Quantum Phase Estimation (IQPE) is used to disentangle c from b.
